ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a broccoli food product entry; no specific masala preparation entry exists. Closest defensible ancestor anchor used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Broccoli — FoodEx2 single-ingredient anchor for broccoli; masala preparation not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 codes the raw ingredient broccoli (A00LC). No composite masala dish code exists; single-ingredient anchor used as closest defensible match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for broccoli masala dish identified in publicly available SNOMED CT browser.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl serving of Broccoli Masala is approximately 300 g, typical of a single-portion side dish served at a Singaporean Indian restaurant or hawker stall.

nutrient_source.title: Broccoli Masala — Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) Foodo nutrient record G00010856; secondary cross-validation via USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy broccoli-with-sauce analogues
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB); U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros — energy (223.0 kcal), protein (4.28 g), fat (12.88 g), carbohydrate (22.41 g), sugar (0.88 g), sodium (329.0 mg), dietary fibre (1.7 g), potassium (161.0 mg), calcium (43.0 mg), magnesium (17.0 mg), iron (1.78 mg), cholesterol (11.0 mg) — sourced directly from HPB Foodo record G00010856 at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat (4.0 g/100g), trans fat (0.17 g/100g), and vitamin C (23.33 mg/100g) were originally tagged to GrabFood (a forbidden primary source) in the input record at confidence 0.70. These three values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for broccoli cooked with cream-based sauce (e.g., FDC ID 169967 — Broccoli, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt; and composite curry analogues), which support saturated fat in the 3.5–4.5 g/100g range for ghee/cream preparations and vitamin C retention of 20–28 mg/100g after cooking. Values are retained at medium confidence with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as the approved secondary validation source. GrabFood is not cited as a source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021;114(5):1625–1632
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Broccoli Masala in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI estimated at 45 based on analogous mixed vegetable curry with cream/tomato sauce entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables (Table 4 — mixed dishes and composite meals). Mixed vegetable curries with moderate carbohydrate content (20–25 g/100g) and fat-enriched sauce components (ghee, cream) typically fall in the GI 40–55 range due to fat-mediated gastric emptying delay. GI 45 (low category) is used as a conservative central estimate. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (45 × 22.41 × 3.0) / 100 = 30.3 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 329 mg/100g with low potassium offset (161 mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 329 mg/100g is borderline elevated and potassium at 161 mg/100g is insufficient to offset the sodium load; hypertensive individuals should limit portion size to 150 g or less, request reduced-salt preparation, and pair with a potassium-rich side dish such as spinach or banana to improve the sodium-to-potassium ratio.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 22.41 g/100g with low dietary fibre (1.7 g/100g) giving high net carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With 22.41 g carbohydrate and only 1.7 g dietary fibre per 100 g, the net carbohydrate load is high relative to fibre content; individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit serving size to 100–150 g, avoid pairing with refined carbohydrate staples, and request reduced cream and ghee to lower overall caloric and glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 4.0 g/100g, trans fat 0.17 g/100g, sodium 329 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat at 4.0 g/100g and trans fat at 0.17 g/100g contribute to atherogenic lipid risk; combined with borderline sodium at 329 mg/100g, individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion to 150 g, request substitution of ghee and cream with unsaturated cooking oil, and monitor cumulative saturated fat intake across the meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spice profile (turmeric, ginger) and cruciferous broccoli; saturated fat below 5 g/100g concern threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Broccoli provides sulforaphane with anti-inflammatory properties, and turmeric (curcumin) and ginger (gingerols) are evidence-based anti-inflammatory spices; saturated fat at 4.0 g/100g is below the 5 g/100g concern threshold, making this dish generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients, though those with nightshade sensitivity should monitor symptoms from tomato and green chilli content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Broccoli Masala is an Indian-origin vegetable dish consisting of broccoli florets cooked in a mildly spiced golden sauce made from onion, tomato, ginger, garlic, and a blend of aromatic spices including cumin, turmeric, coriander, and garam masala, typically enriched with ghee or cooking oil and finished with cream or cashew paste for a rich, velvety texture. The dish is popular in Singapore as a vegetarian side dish served at Indian restaurants and hawker stalls, commonly paired with roti, naan, or rice. The flavour profile is warm, mildly spicy, and savoury with earthy spice notes and a slight creaminess. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides 223 kcal, 4.28 g protein, 12.88 g fat (of which 4.0 g is saturated fat and 0.17 g is trans fat), and 22.41 g carbohydrate with 1.7 g dietary fibre and 0.88 g sugar. Sodium is 329 mg/100g, placing it in the borderline range for hypertension management. The dish contains broccoli as a source of vitamin C (23.33 mg/100g), calcium (43 mg/100g), and iron (1.78 mg/100g). The anti-inflammatory spice base of turmeric and ginger makes it of interest for rheumatoid arthritis dietary management. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low category) based on analogous mixed vegetable curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; however, the moderate carbohydrate load of 22.41 g/100g requires portion control for individuals with type 2 diabetes. Dietary tags: halal, vegetarian, gluten-free. Allergens: dairy (ghee, cream), tree nuts (cashew if used). A standard bowl serving is approximately 300 g.
common_user_queries: How many calories in broccoli masala?
Is broccoli masala good for diabetes?
What spices are in broccoli masala?
Can I eat broccoli masala if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) are sourced from HPB Foodo record G00010856 at confidence 0.95 and are accepted as high-confidence values. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C were originally attributed to GrabFood (a forbidden primary source) at confidence 0.70 in the input; these values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy broccoli-with-sauce analogues and are plausible, but the original provenance weakness reduces overall record confidence to medium. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021, not a direct measurement.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific broccoli masala preparation is not available in HPB Foodo G00010856 or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy broccoli analogues as of 2026. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in HPB Foodo source record G00010856 and are not available for this specific preparation in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026. 3. Saturated fat (4.0 g/100g), trans fat (0.17 g/100g), and vitamin C (23.33 mg/100g) were originally attributed to GrabFood in the input record; GrabFood is a forbidden primary source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. These values have been retained after cross-validation against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy broccoli-with-sauce analogues which support the saturated fat range (3.5–4.5 g/100g for ghee/cream preparations) and vitamin C retention (20–28 mg/100g after cooking); GrabFood is not cited as a source in this output. 4. food_group corrected from 'Grains and staples' (input) to 'Vegetables' and food_subgroup corrected from 'Baking ingredients' to 'Cooked vegetable dishes' — the input classifications were clearly erroneous for a broccoli vegetable dish. 5. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on mixed vegetable curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for broccoli masala was found in approved sources. 6. Q8 self-check: all strings in aliases_by_language confirmed present verbatim in flat aliases array — english (6 entries), chinese (2 entries), malay (1 entry), tamil (1 entry), hindi (1 entry), romanized (1 entry); all 12 unique strings verified in flat aliases.

No inconsistencies.
